herfst.e:
From Middle Dutch herfst, from Old Dutch *hervist, from Proto-West Germanic *harbist, from Proto-Germanic *harbistaz, from Proto-Indo-European *kerp-. The original meaning was “harvest time”, as is seen in herfstmaand.
Cognate with German Herbst, Low German Harvst, West Frisian hjerst, English ha...

het.e: From Middle Dutch dat, which was contracted to 't in usual speech. This form was later interpreted as being the same as the neuter pronoun het (etymology 2, see below), which was contracted in the same way. This then led to the modern merge with het, which some might see as being unetymological.

humor.e:
Borrowed from English humor (US), from Old French humor (“bodily fluid”), from Latin hūmor. Doublet of humeur (“mood, mental state”).
The meaning of humor as in "a sense of amusement" entered Dutch from the US spelling of humour around ~1839.

Kauwgom is dus schadelijk, al helemaal als het niet suiker-vrij is.
Wie ben jij nu helemaal?

helemaal.e: From earlier heeltemael, gheheel te mael. Compare Dutch allemaal, English suffix -meal.

horloge.e: From Middle Dutch orlogie, from Old French orloge, from Latin hōrologium, from Ancient Greek ὡρολόγιον (hōrológion). The current spelling was influenced by Modern French horloge.

examples: Probeer met elke herhaling hoger te komen.
Ik heb deze aflevering al gezien; het is een herhaling.

s.def: a hamlet in Terschelling, Friesland, Netherlands

hee.e: First attested as heed in the 15th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Hee, potentially derived from Old Frisian *hede (“anchorage”).

s.def: wonderful (when referring to a sensation), great
lordly, suzerain (pertaining to a feudal lord)

heerlijk.e: From Middle Dutch heerlijc; compare German herrlich, West Frisian hearlik, Swedish härlig. Equivalent to heer + -lijk.

heroïne.e: From German Heroin, a brandname deposited in 1898 by Bayer.

s.def: a main course, the main dish of a meal

hubert.e: From Hubertus, Latinised form of Hubrecht.

s.def: only used in alle hens aan dek (“all hands on deck”)
only used in in de hens (“on fire”)

examples: Ik heb een hekel aan mijn belastingformulier invullen.

hekel.e: Probably from German Ekel. Initial h- is likely due to a folk etymology.

higgsboson.e: Borrowed from English Higgs boson; see boson.

hypnose.e: Learned borrowing from Ancient Greek ὕπνος (húpnos, “sleep”).

examples: Coordinate term: mem
Mijn heit houdt maar niet op over zijn Friese afkomst.

s.def: analogous part of footwear or another physical entity

hiel.e: From Middle Dutch hiele, from Old Dutch *hela, from Proto-Germanic *hanhilaz.

s.def: to and fro, back and forth
examples: Hij liep heen en weer in de kamer terwijl hij aan het nadenken was.
De schommel bewoog heen en weer onder de boom.
De baron gaf den koetsier een wenk en het rijtuig rolde heen.
Waar ga ik heen?

s.def: gone, outside the boundaries of the norms
examples: Hij was echt ver heen toen.

heen.e: From Middle Dutch henen (“away from here, hence”), from a Proto-West Germanic *hin-, from Proto-Germanic *hiz, and Proto-Germanic *-anē. Cognate with German hin, English hence.

examples: De anderhalvemetereconomie zorgt ervoor dat huisartsen- en ziekenhuiszorg zo georganiseerd moeten worden dat patiënten, letterlijk en figuurlijk, waar dat kan zo veel mogelijk ob afstand worden gehouden tot er een vaccin gevonden is en dat breed wordt toegepast.

s.def: huh; said when surprised or puzzled.
examples: Hè? Wat gebeurde er nou?

s.def: ugh, aww; said when disappointed or annoyed.
examples: Hè, doe niet zo kinderachtig!

s.def: does it?, is it?, right?, eh?; creates a tag question.
examples: Dat was niet zo slim van je, hè?
De reactie van de premier was dat het hartstikke gaaf was.

hartstikke.e: Compound of hart (“heart”) + steek (“stab”). Originally used as an intensifier in the phrase hartstikke dood, usage subsequently spread to other constructions.

examples: Hun huwelijk duurde meer dan vijftig jaar.
Het gelukkige paar vierde hun huwelijk met een groot feest.

huwelijk.e: From Middle Dutch huwelic, huweleec, compound of huwen (“to marry”) + leec (“singing; playing, game”), thus originally referring to the wedding celebration (or less likely the sexual union). Cognate with Middle Low German hillik, Old High German hīleih.

examples: In de kamer voor hem heerste een onbeschrijfelijke wanorde.

heersen.e: From late Middle Dutch hêerschen (“to command, rule”), borrowed from German herrschen (Early New High German). Related to heer.

examples: De schilders gebruikten een hoogwerker om de bovenste verdiepingen van het gebouw te bereiken.
Je moet een speciale training volgen om een hoogwerker te bedienen.

s.def: acronym of Hoe gaat het? (which means how are you doing?)

hypotheek.e: From Latin hypothēca (“pledge, mortgage”), from Ancient Greek ὑποθήκη (hupothḗkē, “warning, pledge”).

examples: De hypotheekrente is de rente die je betaalt op je hypotheeklening.
De hoogte van de hypotheekrente kan variëren afhankelijk van de geldende marktomstandigheden.

hoofdstuk.e: From hoofd- + stuk, calqued from Latin capitulum.

huidig.e: From Middle Dutch hudich, from hude (“today”) + -ich, from Old Dutch hiudo. This word preserves the original vowel that was altered in modern heden.

examples: Uw naam worde geheiligd.

heiligen.e: From Middle Dutch heiligen, from Old Dutch *hēlagon, *heiligon, from Proto-Germanic *hailagōną. Equivalent to heilig (“holy”) + -en.

examples: van hogerhand

handeling.e: From Middle Dutch handelinge, from Old Dutch *handalunga, from Proto-West Germanic *handulungu. By surface analysis, handelen + -ing.

examples: Hij is al jaren bezig met handelen in antiek.
De familie heeft een lange traditie van handelen in textiel.
Je moet snel handelen in noodsituaties.
De politieagent handelde onmiddellijk bij het zien van de verdachte activiteit.

s.def: to handle, to deal with or endure something or someone (particularly emotionally)
examples: Hij kon het volwassen leven niet handelen en ging aan de drugs.

handelen.e: From Middle Dutch handelen, from Old Dutch handalon, from Proto-West Germanic *handulōn, from Proto-Germanic *handlōną. By surface analysis, hand + elen. Ultimately of the same origin as the English borrowing at Etymology 2 below (thus, a doublet of the same).

hell.e: First attested as in hello in the middle of the twelfth century. Presumably a compound of Middle Dutch helle (“lowland, marshy area”) and lo (“light forest on sandy soil”). An alternative interpretation reads the toponym as a compound of Middle Dutch hel (“bright, clear”) and lo (“pool”). Compare...

s.def: bracket (for enclosing text or mathematical symbols)

haak.e: From Middle Dutch hāke, from Old Dutch *hako, from Proto-West Germanic *hakō, from Proto-Germanic *hakô.

haken.e: From Middle Dutch haken. Equivalent to haak + -en.

s.def: hospital, now in particular a military hospital

hospitaal.e: From Middle Dutch hospitael, Old French hospital (Modern French hôpital), from Latin hospitālis (“hospitable”), from hospes (“host, guest”).

s.def: halogen (any element of group 7 of the periodic table)

halogeen.e: Borrowed from New Latin halogenium, from Swedish halogen, both coined by Jöns Jakob Berzelius, a Swedish chemist. Formed from Ancient Greek ἅλς (háls, “salt”) + -geen (“produce”), referring to elements which produce a salt in union with a metal.

s.def: to run fast, race on foot

hardlopen.e: Compound of hard (“hard, fast”) + lopen (“to run”).

s.def: to render hard(er), more resistant etc.; to temper metal; to steel nerves
examples: De geharde veteranen verbeten de pijn zonder jammeren.
Deze stank is niet te harden.

harden.e: From Middle Dutch harden, herden, from Old Dutch *herden, from Proto-West Germanic *hardijan, from Proto-Germanic *hardijaną.

heftig.e: 16th c., borrowed from German heftig, from Middle High German heftec, heiftec, from Old High German heiftig. Can be analyzed as heft (“control”) + -ig (“-y”).

s.def: lizard, non-snake reptilian of the subclass Squamata, especially of the Lacertilia

hagedis.e: From Middle Dutch hagetisse, from Old Dutch *egithassa, from Proto-West Germanic *agiþahsijā (“lizard”).

s.def: a hamlet in Venray, Limburg, Netherlands

a lane, (often double) row of lined-up persons or objects, as for a formal guard of honor inspection

haag.e: Potentially attested as hage in 1292, attested with certainty as Haag in 1899. Derived from haag (“fence, enclosed area”).

hoedanigheid.e: From Middle Dutch hoedanicheit, calque of Latin qualitās. Equivalent to hoedanig + -heid.

ermine, the white fur of the stoat

hermelijn.e: From Middle Dutch hermelijn, a diminutive of hermel, harmel, from Old Dutch *hermilo, itself also a diminutive, of *harmo, from Proto-Germanic *harmô. By surface analysis, hermel + -lijn; hermel (also harmel) survived into early Modern Dutch. Cognate with Middle Low German hermelīn, German Hermelin.

hede.e: From Middle Dutch hēde, eastern variant of herde, heerde, from Proto-West Germanic *heʀdā.

s.def: pronominal adverb form of voor + dit

hooi.e: From Middle Dutch hôoi, from Old Dutch *hōi, from Proto-Germanic *hawją.

hoorn.e: From Middle Dutch horn, from Old Dutch *horn, from Proto-West Germanic *horn, from Proto-Germanic *hurną, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱer-. Compare also horen, from the same origin.

hakken.e: From Middle Dutch hacken, from Old Dutch *hackon, from Proto-Germanic *hakkōną.

henoch.e: Ultimately from Ancient Greek Ἑνώχ (Henṓkh), from Hebrew חֲנוֹךְ.

havik.e:
From Middle Dutch havic, from Old Dutch *havuk, from Proto-West Germanic *habuk.
Compare Low German Haavk, German Habicht, West Frisian hauk, English hawk, Swedish hök, Norwegian hauk. The political sense derived from English hawk.

Ze klommen over het hek om de boomgaard binnen te komen.

hek.e: From Middle Dutch hecke, from Old Dutch *hekka, from Proto-West Germanic *hakkju.

s.def: a herdsman, herd, herder of a flock of animals

herder.e: From Middle Dutch herder, earlier herde from Proto-Germanic *herdō (“herd”) + agent suffix -e; the agent suffix -er replaced -e when the latter fell in disuse as signifying agency.

examples: Coordinate term: kuit (“spawn, roe”)

hom.e: From Middle Dutch homme, identical to homme (“mold”), of uncertain origin, but probably related to Old Norse húm (“dusky, twilight”), from Proto-Germanic *skim- (“to shine-”), which has been compared to Proto-Indo-European *(s)kewH- (“to cover”), but according to the Etymologisch Woordenboek this...

s.def: humane (with regard for the health and well-being of another; compassionate)

hessen.e: From Middle Dutch hessen, from Middle High German Hessen (“Hesse”) (see modern German Hesse).

heffen.e: From Middle Dutch heffen, from Old Dutch heffen, from Proto-West Germanic *habbjan, from Proto-Germanic *habjaną, a class 6 strong verb with j-present, from Proto-Indo-European *kh₂pyéti, from the root *kap- (“to seize”).

hijsen.e:
From late Middle Dutch hisen, hischen. Further etymology uncertain, but possibly imitative.
Related to Middle Low German hissen, German hissen.

helper, aide, assistant, sidekick (also in the masculine gender)

hulp.e: From Middle Dutch hulpe, from Old Dutch hulpa, from Proto-Germanic *hulpiz.

general appearance and/or behaviour of a plant

habitus.e: Borrowed from Latin habitus (“habit”), a noun based on habeō (“have; maintain”).

historisch.e: Borrowed from German historisch, borrowed from Latin historicus, borrowed from Ancient Greek ἱστορικός (historikós). The form borrowed from German replaced earlier forms ending on the equivalent of -lijk. By surface analysis, historie + -isch.

s.def: to host (to perform the role of a host for/at)

hoest.e: From Middle Dutch hoeste, from Old Dutch [Term?], from Proto-West Germanic *hwōstō, from Proto-Germanic *hwōstô.

examples: Deze twee koekjes smaken precies hetzelfde.

herkomst.e: From Middle Dutch hercompst, a derivative of Middle Dutch hercōmen (“to come from, originate from”), equivalent to her (“hither”) + komen (“to come”) + st.

examples: Vorig jaar op 1 januari nam hij afscheid van de hermandad. De 62-jarige muzikant mag nog tot zijn 65e zijn partijtje meeblazen in het politieorkest, maar kiest er nu toch voor om te stoppen.

s.def: to need to, to have to
examples: Je hoeft niet ver te zoeken.

hoeven.e: From Middle Dutch *hoeven, of uncertain origin and formation. Cognates, such as Middle Low German hö̂ven (“to require, need”) and West Frisian hoeve (“to need, want”), are attested much more sparsely, and later, than those of the prefixed forms (e.g. Dutch behoeven). It therefore appears that hoe...

heden.e: From Middle Dutch heden, also hude, huden, from Old Dutch hiudo, a contraction of the instrumental phrase *hiu dago (“on this day”), from Proto-West Germanic *hiu dagu, from Proto-Germanic *hinō dagō (*hiz + *dagaz). The appearance of the vowel -e- is unusual, the expected form in modern Dutch wo...

hanteren.e: From Middle Dutch hanteren, hantieren, from Old French hanter, via Old Norse (or possibly Old English) from Proto-Germanic *haimatjaną.

hoer.e: From Middle Dutch hoere, from Old Dutch *huora, from Proto-Germanic *hōrǭ.

examples: her- + laden (“to load”) → herladen (“to reload”)

her-.e: From southern Middle Dutch her- (“re-, again”). Of uncertain origin, but mostly considered a borrowing from Old French re- or specifically its northern variant er-, from Latin re- (existing as a doublet in the borrowed prefix re-). In certain contexts this prefix touched on the native Middle Dutc...

hoofdzakelijk.e: From hoofdzaak (“main matter”) + -e- + -lijk, perhaps a calque of German hauptsächlich.

heino.e: First attested as heyna in 1245. Etymology unknown.

s.def: a male given name from the Germanic languages, equivalent to English Henry
examples: Altijd kies ik voor de veilige weg. Mijn specialiteit: het sparen van kool én geit. Mijn ouders hadden een vooruitziende blik toen ze mĳ Hendrik noemden: veel braver zul je ze niet snel tegenkomen. 'Kent gij Hendrik niet, die altijd zoo beleefd zijnen hoed afneemt als hij voorbij gaat?' Dat ben ik.

hendrik.e: From Middle Dutch Hendric, Henric, from Old Dutch *Hēmrīk, *Heimrīk, from Proto-West Germanic *Haimarīk, from Proto-Germanic *Haimarīks. Cognates include English Henry, French Henri, German Heinrich, Danish Henrik.

halleluja.e: Borrowed from Latin alleluia, from Hebrew הַלְלוּיָהּ (hal'luyáh, “Praise Yah”).

examples: De kinderopvang werd bevolkt door huilebalken.

examples: De koetsier van de lijkwagen nam zijn huilebalk af, toen hij langs de nabestaanden reed.

hypo-.e: From Ancient Greek ὑπο- (hupo-), combining form of ὑπό (hupó, “under”). Compare sub-.

examples: Ze herstelden het oude huis tot zijn vroegere glorie.
Hij herstelt de computer die is gecrasht.
Na de operatie begon ze snel te herstellen.

hoen.e: From Middle Dutch hoen, from Old Dutch *huon, from Proto-West Germanic *hōn, from Proto-Germanic *hōną. Cognate with German Huhn, Low German Hohn.

handhaven.e: From Middle Dutch hanthaven, from Middle High German hanthaben, itself a calque from Old French maintenir, from Vulgar Latin manūteneō. Likely based also on Middle Dutch handhave (“handle”). Cognate with German handhaben.

hondsdolheid.e: From hond (“dog, canine”) + -s- + dolheid (“madness, frenzy”). First mentioned by Lodewijk Meyer as hondtsdolheidt in 1669, attested since the early eighteenth century.

heimwee.e:
From heim (“home”) + wee (“pain, longing”), calque of German Heimweh; compare nostalgie, which (transmitted via French and New Latin) ultimately represents a calque of the same German word.
The form heim is a Germanism, but nonetheless heimwee prevailed over fully nativized heemwee, which is now ...

hengelen.e: From Middle Dutch hengelen, equivalent to hengel (“fishing rod”) + -en. The fishing sense is influenced by Dutch angel (“fishhook”), Middle Dutch angelen (“to pierce with a hook”). Compare also hengelroede (“fishing rod”) with dialectal or obsolete Dutch angelroede (“fishing rod”), German Angelru...

halverwege.e: Univerbation of halver + wege. Here, halver is the genitive of half, and wege is an inflected form of weg.

examples: Het is maar een kleine hindernis.

a household, domestic unit of cohabitating people

huishouden.e: From huis (“house, home”) + houden (“to keep, maintain”).

hoogstens.e: Derived from hoogst (“highest”) on the model of German höchstens.

hars.e: From Middle Dutch hars, harst, from Middle High German harz (modern German Harz), from Old High German harz, from Proto-West Germanic *hart, from Proto-Germanic *hartą.

Gebruik van ronde haakjes

hilarius.e: From Middle Dutch hilarius, from Latin Hilarius, from Ancient Greek ἱλᾰρός (hilărós).

hoornaar.e: By surface analysis, hoorn + -aar (perhaps referring to its antennae), but likely actually derived from Proto-West Germanic *hurnutu, whence English hornet.

hengst.e: From Middle Dutch henxt, from Old Dutch *hengist, from Proto-West Germanic *hangist, from Proto-Germanic *hangistaz.

houding.e: From houden + -ing. The meaning comes from reflexive 'zich houden'.

examples: Het zal erom spannen.

examples: Minstens 50 van 124 parlementsleden verdwijnen zeker uit Vlaams halfrond

hoefijzer.e: From Middle Dutch hoefisen. Equivalent to hoef (“hoof”) + ijzer (“iron”).

hoef.e:
From Middle Dutch hoef, from Old Dutch *huof, from Proto-Germanic *hōfaz, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱoph₂ós.
Cognate with West Frisian hoef, German Huf, English hoof, Danish hov.

hamel.e: From Middle Dutch hamel, from Old Dutch *hamal, from Proto-Germanic *hamalaz (“mutilated, castrated”). Cognate with German Hammel. Further origin uncertain; perhaps related to ham (“bacon”).

hangmat.e: From Spanish hamaca, from Taíno *hamaka, which changed to ham(m)ak and then, via hangmak, to hangmat, literally “hanging mat”. Modified by influence of hangen (“to hang”) + mat (“mat”). This development is a case of popular etymology. The first attestation of such an adapted form is in a German t...

examples: Wat een hellemoer is dat!

hellemoer.e: From hel (“hell”) + -e- + moer (“mother”), literally “mother of hell”.

Opeens klonk er een hels kabaal vanuit het café. Ik nam aan dat de band dus nog op tijd was gekomen.

harmonie.e: From Middle Dutch harmonia, armonie, from Old French harmonie, from Latin harmonia, from Ancient Greek ἁρμονία (harmonía).

hongerig.e: From Middle Dutch hongerich, from Old Dutch hungerig, from Proto-Germanic *hungragaz, *hungrugaz (“hungry”), equivalent to honger + -ig. Compare German hungrig, English hungry.

heiden.e: From Middle Dutch heidin, a substantivised form of the adjective heidin (“pagan”), from heide (“heath”).

herberg.e: From Middle Dutch herberghe, from Old Dutch *heribergo, from Proto-West Germanic *harjabergu.

handschoen.e: From Middle Dutch antscoen, hantscoe, from Old Dutch *hantscuoh, from Proto-West Germanic *handuskōh. Equivalent to hand + schoen (“shoe, cover”). Cognate with German Handschuh.

hinderen.e: From Middle Dutch hinderen, from Old Dutch *hindaron, from Proto-Germanic *hinderōną.

hysterisch.e: 19th century, borrowed from German hysterisch, which see below.

huig.e: From Middle Dutch huch, huuch, huych, borrowed through Middle High German from Middle Low German hûk. Of uncertain ultimate origin; possibly borrowed from North Germanic, or possibly from a variant of Proto-Germanic *hūbǭ (“head covering”).

houtskool.e: From Middle Dutch houtcole, equivalent to hout (“wood”) + -s- + kool (“coal”).

hoofdletter.e: From Middle Dutch hôoftletter, from Latin littera capitālis. Equivalent to hoofd- (“chief-, major”) + letter.

handdoek.e: From Middle Dutch hantdoec. By surface analysis, hand (“hand”) + doek (“cloth”).

homofobie.e: Probably borrowed from English homophobia. Equivalent homo + -fobie.

halsdoek.e: From Middle Dutch halsdoec. Equivalent to hals + doek. Cognates include German Halstuch.

handpalm.e: From Middle Dutch hantpalme. Equivalent to hand + palm.

hemd.e: From Middle Dutch hemde, hemede, from Old Dutch *hemithi, from Proto-West Germanic *hamiþi (“shirt”). Doublet of chemise and kamisa.

hommel.e:
From Middle Dutch hommel. Likely an onomatopoeic derivation from a verb and the archaic diminutive suffix -el (see Dutch -el).
Cognate with Middle Low German homel, Old High German humbal, German Hummel, West Frisian hommel, Middle English humbil, English humblebee, Norwegian humle, Swedish humla...

honda.e: Borrowed from Japanese 本田 (Honda) or Japanese ホンダ (Honda).

hottentottententententoonstelling.e: From Hottentotten + tenten + tentoonstelling. Extended from Hottentottententoonstelling to make a joke word and tongue-twister, notable for containing the letters "ten" four times in a row. It did not initially have any useful meaning other than the literal meaning implied by its parts.

examples:
Normally used in conjunction with the definite article de or het depending on the gender of what is being referred to.
Die auto is de hunne. — That car is their one. That car is theirs.
Dat huis is het hunne. — That house is their one. That house is theirs.
Dat is de/het hunne. — That is their one. That is theirs.

het geluk is met de dommen.e: Literally, “fortune is with the dumb”. Compare the same in German das Glück ist mit den Dummen alongside Gott ist mit den Dummen (“God is with the dumb”). Probably loosely based on Matthew 5:3: “Blessed are the poor in spirit.”

From Middle Dutch hovesch, and thus a doublet of hoofs (“courtly”). Originally a western variant form.

examples: Het Standaard Model van de elementaire deeltjes beschrijft de elementaire massa deeltjes, zoals quarks en elektronen, en de deeltjes die de krachten tussen die deeltjes overbrengen, zoals het foton voor de elektromagnetische kracht. Het Standaard Model is wiskundig correct als wordt aangenomen dat de deeltjes geen massa hebben. We weten dat er massa in het heelal is, dus er is een uitbreiding van het model bedacht die deeltjes massa kan geven. Die uitbreiding postuleert het bestaan van een veld dat de deeltjes koppelt aan het vacuüm. Bij die kracht hoort het nog niet aangetoonde Higgs deeltje.

* (Zuid-Holland) Compound of hoog (“high, elevated”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”). Formerly an i...

het bildt.e: First attested as een wtlant, gheheten bil, dat aengheworpen is buten dycs in 1398. Derived from Old Frisian bil (“alluvial land, low-lying pasture next to a watercourse”) with epenthetic -t.

s.def: Harlingen (a city and municipality of Friesland, Netherlands)

harlingen.e:
First attested as herlinge in 1232-1233. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Harilo (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-. The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.
See also West Frisian Harns.

s.def: Heerenveen (a village and municipality of Friesland, Netherlands)

heerenveen.e: First attested as Sheerenfeen in 1579. From heer (“gentleman”) + -en- + veen (“peat bog, marsh”). The settlement was named after the owners of the company which developed the land around the future settlement.

s.def: Harderwijk (a city and municipality of Gelderland, Netherlands)
a hamlet in Opmeer, North Holland, Netherlands

harderwijk.e: * (Gelderland) First attested as harderewich and herderewich in 1231. Etymology uncertain. A compound formed from either herder (“shepherd”) or harde (“solid, hard soil”) and wijk (“settlement, village”) is one possibility. Derivations from a shallow area near the city called Het Harde or Old Dut...

s.def: Hattem (a city and municipality of Gelderland, Netherlands)

hattem.e:
First attested as hatheim around 800-850. Compound derived from the tribal name of the Hatti and heem (“home, settlement”).

s.def: Heerde (a village and municipality of Gelderland, Netherlands)

heerde.e: First attested as herodna in 838. Likely derived from Proto-Germanic *harud (“forested heights”) with locative -n- suffix. Compare Hierden, Harderwijk and 't Harde.

s.def: Heumen (a village and municipality of Gelderland, Netherlands)

heumen.e: First attested as homen in 1188. Etymology uncertain, although the origins of the name are likely Germanic. Perhaps derived from Middle Dutch homme (“mould”) (compare huim (“foetid air”)) and Old Norse húm (“twilight, dusk”). More at hom (“milt”).

hippogrief.e: Ultimately from Italian ippogrifo, coined by Ludovico Ariosto.

hoefijzerneus.e: Bahuvrihi compound of hoefijzer (“horseshoe”) + neus (“nose”), calque of German Hufeisennase.

hermeneutiek.e: Borrowed from German Hermeneutik, ultimately from Ancient Greek ἐρμηνευτικός (ermēneutikós). Equivalent to hermeneut + -iek.

heerhugowaard.e: First attested as die waerd in 1319. Compound of heer (“lord”), Hugo (a personal name) and Middle Dutch waert (“land next to water, riverine island, polder complex surrounded by water”). hazennest.e: First attested as die hasennest in 1511. Derived from hazennest (“hare's burrow”).

hooghout.e: First attested as Hooghoutsche Tiend in 1866. Compound of hoog (“high, elevated”) and hout (“rising deciduous forest”).

het goirke.e: First attested as dat goerken in 1435. Derived from a diminutive form of goor (“swamp, marsh”).

huibeven.e: First attested as Huibe ven in 1760. Compound of either Huib (a personal name) or dialectal huib (“owl”) and ven (“small pool in a forest or heath”).

s.def: a hamlet in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands

het sas.e: First attested as Het Sas in 1838. Derived from sas (“lift-lock”).

hondseind.e: First attested as op thonseynt in 1715. Compound of hond (“dog”) and eind (“end, edge”).

houtgoor.e: First attested as opt houtghoir in 1540. Compound of hout (“rising deciduous forest”) and goor (“swampy forest ground”).

het oosteind.e: First attested as Op het Oosteynd in 1697. Compound of oost (“east, eastern-”) and eind (“end, edge”).

heerle.e: First attested as in herelle in 1277. Likely derived from Proto-Germanic *harila (“small sandy ridge”), related to *harugaz (“grove, sanctuary, halidom”). Compare Heel, Heerlen and Heers.

haiink.e: First attested as haghedunc in 1285. Contracted compound of Middle Dutch hage (“fence, enclosed area”) and donk (“sandy hill protruding above a swamp”). Compare Hooidonk.

a hamlet in Roosendaal, North Brabant, Netherlands

hoevestein.e: First attested as Hoevesteyn in 1636. From hoeve (“farmstead”) + -stein (“stone building, castle, manor”).

hollandsdiep.e: Attested as Hollandsch-Diep in 1936. Named after the Hollandsch Diep river.

halderberge.e: Potentially derived from a compound of haar (“sandy ridge”) and lo (“light forest”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). An alternative hypothesis posits a derivation from Proto-Germanic *halu- (“sloping”) and berg. The name was chosen by vote by local history organisations from the municipalities ...

s.def: a village in Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

helwijk.e: First attested as de Hel in 1747. Compound of hel (“hell”) and wijk (“village, settlement”). Named after the De Hel Fortress. The French troops which occupied it in 1811 translated its name to enfer (“hell”), and the name was translated back to Dutch for the settlement that sprung up around it.

s.def: a village in Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

heijningen.e: First attested as die heyninge in 1467. Derived from Middle Dutch heyninge (“enclosure, fence”).

s.def: a hamlet in Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

hoekske.e: Attested as Hoekske in 1936. Derived from a diminutive form of hoek (“corner”). The settlement was named for its location in a corner of the Moerdijk Polder.

heense molen.e: First attested as [molen] omtrent de Heense in 1622. Derived from the toponym De Heen and molen (“windmill”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands

halsteren.e: First attested as halst in 1272. Compound of Proto-Germanic *halha (“spur of a highland”) and Middle Dutch stert (“long, narrow ridge”). Compare De Asterd.

s.def: a hamlet in Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands

heihoefke.e: First attested as t Heihoefke in 1838-1857. Compound of heide (“heath, heathland”) and a diminutive form of hoeve (“farmstead”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands

het fort.e: Derived from fort (“fortress”). Named after the Bergen op Zoom Fortress.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands

het glacis.e: First attested as bedekte Wegen en Glacis in 1740. Derived from glacis (“glacis”). Named after a section of Bergen op Zoom's fortifications.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Woensdrecht, North Brabant, Netherlands

hoogerheide.e: First attested as die hoeghe heide in 1319. Compound of hoog (“high, upper”) and heide (“heath, heathland”). So named to distinguish the settlement from Nederheide.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Woensdrecht, North Brabant, Netherlands

huijbergen.e: First attested as in huybergen in 1264. Etymology uncertain. Potentially a compound of dialectal hui (“owl”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). Compare Huij.

a hamlet in Tholen, Zeeland, Netherlands

het oudeland.e:
* (Borsele) Attested as Oudelants P. around 1750. Derived from oudland (“land which has not been reclaimed, already existing land”).
* (Tholen) Attested as t Oudeland in 1343. Derived from oudland (“land which has not been reclaimed, already existing land”).

s.def: a former village and former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

haamstede.e: Named after Haamstede, currently part of Burgh-Haamstede.

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

hoogelande.e:
First attested as hogeland in 1189. Compound of hoog (“high, elevated”) and land (“land”).
See also Zealandic Hoôgelande, Oôgelande.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands

heinkenszand.e:
First attested as heynekijnssand in 1351. Derived from a diminutive form of Middle Dutch Hendrik (a personal name) and zand (“sand bar”).
See also Zealandic Eintjeszand, 'Eintjeszand.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands

hoedekenskerke.e:
First attested as hoedekinskerke and oedekinskerke around 1280. Compound of a diminutive form of Middle Dutch Odo (a personal name) and kerk (“church”).
See also Zealandic Oedjeskerke.

s.def: a village in Reimerswaal, Zeeland, Netherlands

hansweert.e:
First attested as hannekinswaerde in 1353. Derived from a diminutive form of Middle Dutch Johannes (a personal name) and waard (“land next to water, riverine island”).
See also Zealandic Answest.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

hengstdijk.e: First attested as hencsdic in 1161. Compound of Old Dutch Henge (a personal name) and dijk (“levee”). The first element is not hengst (“stallion”).

s.def: a former village and former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

hontenisse.e:
First attested as guntenesse in 1182-1183. Compound of the hydronym Honte and Old Dutch nesse (“headland, spit”).
The municipality was named after the village.

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

halfeind.e: Attested as Halfeind in 1936. Compound of half (“half”) and eind (“end”). Named for its location midway between Clinge and Nieuw-Namen.

s.def: a hamlet in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

hasjesstraat.e: Attested as Hasjesstraat in 1838-1857. The settlement was named after its location next to the eponymous street.

s.def: a hamlet in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

hoek van de dijk.e: Derived from hoek (“corner”) and dijk (“levee”). The settlement was named after its location next to the corner of two levees.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

hugersluys.e: Attested as Huigersluis in 1740. Compound of Hugo (a personal name) and sluis (“lock, sluice”). The lock after which the neighbourhood was named was built by Hugo van Steenland.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

hoofdplaat.e:
First attested as Hooge Plaet in 1665. Compound of hoog (“high, elevated”) and plaat (“sand bar”). The first element was gradually reinterpreted to hoofd (“head”).
See also Zealandic Dwòfdplaote, Dôôfplaote.

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

heille.e:
First attested as heile in 1188. Potentially a compound of Old Dutch hegi (“fence”) and lo (“light forest”).
See also Zealandic Eile, 'Eile.

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

hoogeweg.e: Attested as De hooge weg in 1838-1857. Compound of hoog (“high, elevated”) and weg (“way, road”).

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

het zwindorp.e: Compound of the hydronym Zwin and dorp (“village”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zeewolde, Flevoland, Netherlands

harderhaven.e: Blend of Harderwijk + haven (“harbour, port”). The settlement was named for its close proximity to Harderwijk.

s.def: a hamlet in Kampen, Overijssel, Netherlands

hogeweg.e: First attested as Hoogenweg and Hoogeweg (De) in 1899. Compound of hoog (“high”) and weg (“way, road”). Originally the name of a levee.

a hamlet in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

heetveld.e: First attested as Heedveld in 1830-1855. Derived from a dialectal variant of heideveld (“heathland”).

hamingen.e: First attested as Haeminge in 1594. Derived either from Old Dutch Hame (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- or from Middle Dutch *haam (“sack-shaped fishing net”) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-.

s.def: a hamlet in Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

haerst.e: First attested as haerste in 1381-1383. Likely derived from a variant of Middle Dutch horst (“overgrown elevated place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

harculo.e: First attested as herkeloe in 1216-1227. Potentially a compound of Old Dutch Hariko (a personal name) and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

herfte.e: First attested as heruten around 1310. Presumably derived from Proto-Germanic *harwja- (“flax”) suffixed with the collectivising suffix *-iþi.

s.def: a hamlet in Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

hoog zuthem.e: First attested as suthem around 1300. Derived from hoog (“high, upper”) and a compound of Middle Dutch suut (“south-, southern”) and heem (“home, settlement”). The element hoog was added to distinguish the settlement from Laag Zuthem.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

hanzeland.e: Compound of the term Hanze and land (“land”). The name was chosen as a reference to Zwolle's membership of the Hanseatic League.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

holtenbroek.e: First attested as Holtenbroick in 1515. Compound of the toponym Holten and broek (“marsh, marshland”).

s.def: a village in Dalfsen, Overijssel, Netherlands

hoonhorst.e: First attested as de honhorst in 1283. Compound of Middle Dutch hogen (“elevated”) and horst (“overgrown elevated place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Dalfsen, Overijssel, Netherlands

hessum.e: First attested as hossem and hesne in 1328. Etymology uncertain. Ostensibly a compound of a personal name and heem (“home, settlement”), but the second attestation of 1328 casts doubt on this etymology. Compare Dalfsen, Oudleusen and Welsum.

s.def: a hamlet in Ommen, Overijssel, Netherlands

hoogengraven.e: A clipping of Hooge Graven Brug attested 1830–1855, from Hooge Graven + brug (“bridge”).

s.def: a village in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

heemse.e: First attested as heymiss in 1240. Etymology unknown. Proposed etymologies include a derivation from heem (“home, settlement”) and from Old Dutch Heimezo (a personal name). Compare Heemskerk.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

heemsermars.e: Compound of the toponym Heemse and mars (“land next to water, pasture”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands
a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

hardenbergerveld.e: Compound of the toponym Hardenberg and veld (“field”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

heemserveen.e: Compound of the toponym Heemse and veen (“peat bog”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

holtheme.e: First attested as to holtheem in 1381-1383. Compound of Middle Dutch holt (“rising deciduous forest”) and heem (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

holthone.e: First attested as holton in 1188. Compound of Old Dutch holt (“rising deciduous forest”) and ho (“elevated place”). Compare Meddo, Vollenhove and Wiene.

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

hoogenweg.e: First attested as Hooge Weg in 1830-1855. Compound of hoog (“high, elevated”) and weg (“road, way”). Named after a nearby road.

s.def: a hamlet in Twenterand, Overijssel, Netherlands

hallerhoek.e: First attested as to halle in 1381-1383. Compound of the microtoponym Halle (a former farmstead) and hoek (“isolated place, sector”).

s.def: a hamlet in Wierden, Overijssel, Netherlands

hoge hexel.e: First attested as heghelsclo and hegheslo in 1381-1383. Derived from hoog (“high, elevated”) and a compound of an uncertain first element and lo (“light forest”). Compare Hexel.

s.def: a hamlet in Wierden, Overijssel, Netherlands

huurne.e: First attested as Hurene in 1830-1855. Derived from a dialectal variant of horn (“jutting piece of land, corner”).

hellendoorn.e: First attested as hellender in 1275-1280. Perhaps derived from Middle Dutch holendere (“elder, Sambucus nigra”). A compound of Middle Dutch hel (“low-lying”) and doorn (“thorn bush”) is also possible. Compare Haalderen and Halder.

a hamlet in Tubbergen, Overijssel, Netherlands

haarle.e:
* (Hellendoorn) First attested as haerle in 1367. Compound of Middle Dutch haar (“sandy ridge”) and lo (“light forest”).
* (Tubbergen) First attested as villa harlo in 1244. Compound of Middle Dutch haar (“sandy ridge”) and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hellendoorn, Overijssel, Netherlands

hankate.e: First attested as hagencote in 1383. Compound of Middle Dutch Hage (a personal name) or haag (“fence, enclosed area”) and the dative singular form of Middle Dutch cote (“cottage, shack, small farm”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hellendoorn, Overijssel, Netherlands

hexel.e: First attested as t Hacksel in 1665. Likely a compound of an unclear first element and lo (“light forest”). Compare Hoge Hexel.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Raalte, Overijssel, Netherlands

het raan.e: First attested as roiden around 1310. Derived from a contraction of the dative plural form of Middle Dutch rode (“land cleared of trees”). Compare Raar, Raren, Rha, Rhaan, Rhoon and Roden.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Raalte, Overijssel, Netherlands
a hamlet in Olst-Wijhe, Overijssel, Netherlands

hengforden.e: First attested as hengorden around 1300. Etymology uncertain. Unlikely to be a compound of a term cognate to hangen (“hang”) (here designating an incline) and voorde (“ford, crossing”). The second element may be woerd (“artificial hill meant for habitation”). Compare Hengelder, Hengelo and Hengev...

s.def: a hamlet in Olst-Wijhe, Overijssel, Netherlands

herxen.e: First attested as herxze, hercsem, herxe and herchse around 1310. Etymology unknown. Not a compound of a personal name and heem (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

het oostrik.e: First attested as Oesterick around 1602. From a contracted compound of ooster- (“eastern, east-”) + wijk (“village, settlement”). Originally the name of a farm.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

het jeurlink.e: Derived from Old Dutch Jordilo (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing. Named after a former farmstead.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

het fetlaer.e: First attested as Fittelaar, Fetlaar, Fettelaar, Fitlaar and Vittelaar in the 19th century. Compound of dialectal vitte (“pin, mold”) and laar (“intensively cultivated forest”). Named after a former farmstead.

s.def: a hamlet in Rijssen-Holten, Overijssel, Netherlands

helhuizen.e: First attested as HELHUIZEN in 1844. Compound of hel (“slope, incline”) and the plural form of huis (“house”). Compare Hell, Helwerd, Holwerd and Hulhuizen.

s.def: a hamlet in Rijssen-Holten, Overijssel, Netherlands

holterbroek.e: Compound of the toponym Holten and broek (“marsh, marshland”).

hof van twente.e: Derived from hof (“estate”) and the toponym Twente. The name of the new municipality was chosen because of its largely rural character and the presence of multiple large estates within its boundaries.

s.def: a village in Hof van Twente, Overijssel, Netherlands

hengevelde.e: First attested as hengheuelde in 1188. Potentially a compound of either henge (“fence, enclosure”) or a term cognate to hangen (“hang”) (here referring to an incline) and veld (“field”). Compare Hengelder, Hengelo and Hengforden.

s.def: a hamlet in Hof van Twente, Overijssel, Netherlands

herike.e: First attested as hedereke in 1188. Etymology uncertain. Potential etymologies include a derivation from Old Dutch Hadurik (a personal name), a derivation from a collectivised form of hederik (“charlock (Rhamphospermum arvense, syns. Sinapis arvensis, etc.)”) and a compound derivation from heide ...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hof van Twente, Overijssel, Netherlands

hooijerinkses.e: First attested as hoyerinc to delden by der dorpe in 1381-1383. Compound of a toponym derived from Middle Dutch Hoyer (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and es (“farmland next to a village”).

s.def: a village in Borne, Overijssel, Netherlands

hertme.e: First attested as die mole toe hertmen in 1315. Etymology unknown. Compare Bathmen, Berkum, Besthmen, Diermen, Fortmond, Giethmen, Helpman, Ratum, Reutum, Thamen, Wachtum and Wijthmen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

haghoek.e: Attested as t Hag in 1867. Compound of dialectal hag (“hedge, hedgerow”) and hoek (“isolated place, sector”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almelo, Overijssel, Netherlands
a village in Tubbergen, Overijssel, Netherlands

harbrinkhoek.e: First attested as HARBRINK, Harberink, Harberinkshoek of Harbrinkshoek in 1844. Compound of Middle Dutch Harbert (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and hoek (“isolated place, sector”).

s.def: a hamlet in Tubbergen, Overijssel, Netherlands

hezingen.e: First attested as in hasungum in 799. Derived from Old Dutch Haso (a personal name) suffixed with the rare collectivising suffix -ung-.

s.def: a hamlet in Dinkelland, Overijssel, Netherlands

het stift.e: First attested as Stift te Weerselo in the 18th century. Derived from stift (“monastery, ecclesiastical foundation”). Originally the name of a farm, which was in turn named after a former monastery.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Enschede, Overijssel, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Enschede, Overijssel, Netherlands

heuwkamp.e: Compound of Dutch Low Saxon heuw (“hay”) and kamp (“enclosed piece of land”). Named after a street, which was named in turn after a field.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Enschede, Overijssel, Netherlands

helmerhoek.e: First attested as tot helmyng in 1456. Compound of Middle Dutch Helm (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and hoek (“isolated place, sector”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hengelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

hengelose es.e: First attested as Hengelosche Esch in 1867. Derived from the toponym Hengelo and es (“farmland next to a village”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hengelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

hasseler es.e: First attested as Hasseler Esch in 1830-1855. Derived from the toponym Hasselo and es (“farmland next to a village”).

s.def: a hamlet in Haaksbergen, Overijssel, Netherlands

harmöle.e: First attested as harremole in 1188. Likely a compound of Proto-Germanic *harwja (“flax”) and *molî (“mill”). See also Harreveld.

s.def: a hamlet in Haaksbergen, Overijssel, Netherlands

honesch.e: First attested as Honesch in 1665. Compound of Middle Dutch ho (“high, elevated”) and es (“farmland next to a village”). The toponym contrasts with Zienesch.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Haaksbergen, Overijssel, Netherlands

hassinkbrink.e: First attested as hassinck in 1472. Compound of Old Dutch Haso (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and brink (“pasture, village green, farmland”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Kampen, Overijssel, Netherlands

hanzewijk.e: Compound of Hanze (“Hanseatic League”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). Kampen was a long-standing member of the Hanseatic League.

homeruskwartier.e: Compound of Homerus (a personal name) and kwartier (“quarter, neighbourhood”). Named after the Greek poet Homer. The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous poets.

havenkwartier.e: Compound of haven (“harbour”) and kwartier (“quarter, neighbourhood”).

heiakkers.e: Compound of hei (“heath, heathland”) and the plural form of akker (“farmland”).

haagpoort.e: Compound of the toponym Princenhage and poort (“gate”).

a neighbourhood of Oss, North Brabant, Netherlands

hertogenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of hertog (“duke”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after various dukes.

hogenkamp.e: Compound of an inflected form of hoog (“high”) and kamp (“enclosed piece of land”).

See also West Frisian Huzum.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

hollanderwijk.e: Compound of Hollander (“inhabitant of Holland”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The toponym refers to the Hollanderdijk, a levee constructed in the 13th century.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

heechterp.e: First attested as Terp in 1511. Borrowed from West Frisian Heechterp, derived in turn from a compound of heech (“high, elevated”) and terp (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

harich.e: First attested as with certainty as harich in 1245. Borrowed from West Frisian Harich, derived in turn from Proto-Germanic *harug (“rockpile, holy place”). Compare Hargen, Kethel and Harrow.

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

haskerhorne.e: First attested as hascherahorna in 1333. Calque of West Frisian Haskerhoarne, a compound of the toponym Aldehaske and horne (“corner, bend”).

s.def: a hamlet in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

huisterheide.e: Derived from a contraction of huis (“house”), an inflected form of te (“at”) and heide (“heath, heathland”). Originally the name of an inn.

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hidaard.e: First attested as hedawere in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Hidaard, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Heda (a personal name) and werd (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hennaard.e: Potentially attested as hanwurf [read: hanwurt] in 944, attested with certainty as hernawort in 1319. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian herna (“corner”) and wert (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an older version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hartwerd.e: First attested as hertwarth in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Hartwert, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Harte (a personal name) and wert (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

heeg.e: First attested with certainty as hagakerke in 1245. Borrowed from West Frisian Heech, derived in turn from a compound of Proto-Germanic *hagan (“bush, hedge”) and kerk (“church”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hemelum.e: First attested as hemelem around 1243-1245. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Hemele (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hichtum.e: First attested as hetlum [read: hectum] in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Hichtum, potentially derived from Old Frisian *Higt (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hieslum.e: First attested as hasalon and hasulun around 825-842. Borrowed from West Frisian Hieslum, probably derived in turn from the dative plural form of Old Frisian hasal (“hazel, Corylus avellana”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hommerts.e: First attested as humerke in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian De Hommerts, potentially derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Huga (a personal name) or the demonym Hugas and merke (“boundary, borderland, delineated area”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

het heidenschap.e: First attested as Heijdenscip and Heijdenscop in 1511. Calque of West Frisian It Heidenskip, derived from heidenskip (“overgrown area”).

s.def: Hansard, member of the Hanseatic League
a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hidaarderzijl.e: First attested as Hidaarder Zyl in the 18th century. Calque of West Frisian Hidaardersyl, derived from a compound of the toponym Hidaard and syl (“lift-lock”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hoekens.e: First attested as huckens in 1451. Borrowed from West Frisian Hoekens.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

harkezijl.e: First attested as Harckzyl in 1664. Calque of West Frisian Harkesyl, a compound of Harke (a personal name) and syl (“discharge sluice”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hayum.e: First attested as Hayum in 1515. Borrowed from West Frisian Haaium, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Hadinge (a surname) or Haie (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

het vliet.e: First attested as to da fliete in 1426. Calque of West Frisian It Fliet, derived in turn from Old Frisian fliāt (“running watercourse”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hiddum.e: First attested as Hiddum and Hiddema terp in 1543. Borrowed from West Frisian Hiddum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Hidde (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

hornsterburen.e: First attested as Hornster Buiren in 1718. Calque of West Frisian Hoarnsterbuorren, a compound of horn (“corner of a levee”) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

het hooghout.e: First attested as Draayhout in 1718. Borrowed from West Frisian 't Heechhout, derived from heechhout (“tall, narrow bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

hatsum.e: First attested as Hastzen in 1511. Borrowed from West Frisian Hatsum, potentially derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Hette (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

herbaijum.e: First attested as herbadingum in the 13th century. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Herbad (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hēm (“home, settlement”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

hitzum.e: First attested as hitsem in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Hitsum, derived from a compound of a familiar form of Old Frisian Hidde (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

holprijp.e: First attested in 1433. Borrowed from West Frisian Holpryp.

s.def: a hamlet in Terschelling, Friesland, Netherlands

horp.e: First attested as Horp in 1615. Etymology uncertain.

s.def: a former municipality of Friesland, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Goeree-Overflakkee, South Holland, Netherlands

herkingen.e:
First attested as herkinghe around 1420-1421. Derived from either Middle Dutch Herke (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- or a hydronym derived from or cognate to Middle Dutch *harken (“make a harsh noise”).
See also Zealandic Herken.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Voorne aan Zee, South Holland, Netherlands

heveringen.e: Attested as de Rokanjesche Heveringen in 1769. Derived from the plural form of dialectal hevering (“type of elevated sandy land”).

s.def: a town and former municipality of Voorne aan Zee, South Holland, Netherlands

hellevoetsluis.e: Attested as Helvoet Sluys in 1639. Compound of the toponym Hellevoet (derived from a compound of the hydronym Helle and voet (“foot, bottom”)) and sluis (“lock, sluice”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Voorne aan Zee, South Holland, Netherlands

hoonaart.e: Attested as Den Hoonaart in 1697. Presumably derived from a compound of hogen (“elevated, upper-”) and aard (“farmland, tilled land”).

s.def: a city and former municipality of Nissewaard, South Holland, Netherlands

heenvliet.e: First attested as heenvlite in 1254. Compound of Middle Dutch heen (“sea clubrush, Bolboschoenus maritimus”) and vliet (“watercourse”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Nissewaard, South Holland, Netherlands

hekelingen.e: First attested as hekelinghe in 1285. Likely derived from Middle Dutch *hekel (“hill, elevated land”) or *heukel suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Nissewaard, South Holland, Netherlands

hoogwerf.e: Attested as Hoogwerf in 1874. Compound of hoog (“high, elevated”) and werf (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a town and municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

hendrik-ido-ambacht.e: First attested as henrick iden ambocht in 1340. Compound of the name of Hendrik Ye Wittenzoon and Middle Dutch ambacht (“type of fiefdom”). Wittenzoon was the scion of a banking family which financed the land reclamation projects the current village stands on; he received the fiefdom in 1376.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Zwijndrecht, South Holland, Netherlands

heerjansdam.e: First attested as haren heyenland in 1323. Compound of Middle Dutch here (“lord”), Jan (a personal name) and dam (“dam”). The current name of the village refers to Jan van Roozendaal, a nobleman who was granted the village in 1368.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Zwijndrecht, South Holland, Netherlands

heer oudelands ambacht.e: Attested as Oudelant in 1573. Derived from heer (“lord”), Oudeland (a surname) and ambacht (“type of fiefdom”). Named after Pieter Oudeland, a nobleman from Dordrecht who was one of the financiers of the land reclamation project which created the land the former fiefdom stood on and the current n...

s.def: an island and municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

hoeksche waard.e: Derived from the toponym Puttershoek (formerly called Hoecke) and waard (“riverine island”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Hoeksche Waard, South Holland, Netherlands

heinenoord.e: First attested as heynekens orde in 1312. Compound of a diminutive form of Middle Dutch Hein (a personal name) and oert (“land protruding into or bordering water”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hoeksche Waard, South Holland, Netherlands

hitsertse kade.e: First attested as dscarden an den weerf and scarden an der warf in 1305. Derived from the hydronym Hitsert (a former channel) and kade (“quay, wharf”).

s.def: a municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

hardinxveld-giessendam.e: Compound of the toponyms Hardinxveld and Giessendam.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

hardinxveld.e: First attested as herdingfelde in 1105. Derived from a compound of Old Dutch *Hardo (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and veld (“field”). Originally the name of a single village, currently split in Boven-Hardinxveld and Neder-Hardinxveld.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Gorinchem, South Holland, Netherlands

hematiet.e: Ultimately from Ancient Greek αἱματίτης (haimatítēs), from Ancient Greek αἷμα (haîma, “blood”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Molenlanden, South Holland, Netherlands

hoogblokland.e: First attested as blocland in 1331. Compound of hoog (“high, elevated”) and Middle Dutch blokland (“land without access to nearby roads”). The element hoog was added to distinguish the settlement from Laagblokland.

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Molenlanden, South Holland, Netherlands

hofwegen.e: First attested as hofwegen in 1369. Potentially derived from the plural form of Middle Dutch hofwech (“road running next to a homestead”).

s.def: a hamlet in Molenlanden, South Holland, Netherlands

hoge giessen.e: Attested as Hooge Giessen in 1840. Derived from hoog (“high, elevated”) and the hydronym Giessen.

s.def: a city and former municipality of Krimpenerwaard, South Holland, Netherlands

haastrecht.e: First attested as havekesdret in 1108. Compound of Old Dutch Haveke (a personal name) and dreht (“ferry, watercourse”).

s.def: a hamlet in Krimpenerwaard, South Holland, Netherlands

hoogedijk.e: Compound of hoog (“high, elevated”) and dijk (“levee”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zuidplas, South Holland, Netherlands

hollevoeterbrug.e: Attested as Holvoeters Oph. brug in 1839-1859. Potentially a compound of a nickname or the name of an inn and brug (“bridge”).

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Delft, South Holland, Netherlands

hof van delft.e: First attested as in curta nostra de delf in 1251. Derived from Middle Dutch hof (“farmstead, homestead”) and the toponym Delft.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Rijswijk, South Holland, Netherlands

huis te lande.e: Derived from huis (“house”) and land (“land”). Originally the name of a mansion.

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Midden-Delfland, South Holland, Netherlands

hodenpijl.e: First attested as hodenpile in 1260. Etymology unknown. Potentially a compound of an unclear first element and Middle Dutch pyl (“pointed pole, beam”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Vlaardingen, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Gouda, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Vlaardingen, South Holland, Netherlands

hoevenbuurt.e: Both compounds of the plural form of hoeve (“farmstead, homestead”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). Many of the neighbourhoods' street names end in hoeve.

s.def: a village in Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

hoek van holland.e: Attested as den Hoek van Holland in 1769. Derived from hoek (“sharp bend, corner”) and the toponym Holland.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

heijplaat.e: Attested as Heiplaat (Tuindorp) in 1936. Compound of hei (“heath, heathland”) and plaat (“sand bar visible at low tide”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

hillesluis.e: Attested as Hillesluisdijk in 1845. Compound of the name of the polder Hille (derived from hil (“hill, elevated place”)) and sluis (“sluice, lock”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood and former municipality of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

hillegersberg.e: First attested as rotta in 1028. Potentially a compound of the name of Hildegard of Flanders (wife of Count Dirk II of Holland) and Old Dutch berg (“hill, elevated place”). Originally named after the river Rotte.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

honderd en tien morgen.e: Derived from honderd (“hundred”), tien (“ten”) and morgen (“unit of land measurement”). Originally the name of a polder. Compare Duizend Morgen, Tiengemeten, Twaalfmorgen and Zevenmorgen.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

hoogvliet.e: Attested as Hoochvliet in 1639. Compound of hoog (“high, elevated”) and vliet (“running watercourse”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

hordijkerveld.e: Attested as hordijk in 1446. Compound of the toponym Hordijk (potentially derived from a compound of Middle Dutch horn (“corner, sharp bend”) and dyc (“levee”) or, alternatively, from *hordendijk (“levee reinforced with osier screens”)) and veld (“field”). Hordijk was an independent hamlet before...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

homerusbuurt.e: Compound of the name of Homer and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous authors and poets.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

het lage land.e: Derived from laag (“low”) and land (“land”). The neighbourhood was named for its low elevation.

s.def: a village in Westland, South Holland, Netherlands

honselersdijk.e: First attested as hontsolerdike, hontseler diec and honselerdike in 1280-1287. Compound of the toponym Hontsele and Middle Dutch dyc (“levee”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westland, South Holland, Netherlands

heenweg.e: Attested as Heenweg in 1839-1859. Compound of the toponym Heen (derived from heen (“sea clubrush, Bulboschoenus maritimus”)) and weg (“way, road”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Westland, South Holland, Netherlands

hoge woerd.e: Attested as Hooge Woert in 1712. Derived from hoog (“high, elevated”) and woerd (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The toponym contrasts with Lage Woerd and Midden Woerd.

s.def: a hamlet in The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands

haagoord.e: Allegedly a compound of the toponym Den Haag and oord (“place”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands

houtwijk.e: Compound of the toponym Houtweg and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). Named after a nearby street.

s.def: a neighbourhood of The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands

huygenspark.e: Compound of Huijgens (a surname) and park (“park”). Named after Constantijn Huygens.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leiden, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Leiden, South Holland, Netherlands

haagweg.e: Compound of the toponym Den Haag and weg (“way, road”). Named after a nearby road.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leiden, South Holland, Netherlands

hoge mors.e: First attested as op den merse in oestghester ambochte in 1283. Derived from hoog (“high, elevated”) and a variant of Middle Dutch mersc (“land next to water, swampland, pasture”). The element hoog was added to distinguish the area from Lage Mors.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leiden, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Oegstgeest, South Holland, Netherlands

haaswijk.e: Attested as HAASWIJK in 1844. Compound of haas (“hare, Lepus europaeus”) and wijk (“village, settlement”). Originally the name of a farm, which was named in turn after a former mansion.

s.def: a neighbourhoods of Katwijk, South Holland, Netherlands

hoornes.e: Attested as hoorn-nes in 1615. Compound of horn (“protruding bend, corner”) and nes (“headland, spit”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Teylingen, South Holland, Netherlands

hoogh teylingen.e: Deliberately archaising spelling of hoog (“high, elevated”) and the toponym Teijlingen.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Kaag en Braassem, South Holland, Netherlands

hoogmade.e: First attested as hoghe made in 1280-1287. Compound of Middle Dutch hooch (“high, elevated”) and made (“meadow, pasture”).

s.def: a village in Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

hazerswoude-dorp.e: First attested as hadewartswoude and hadewertswoude in 1280-1287. Compound of Middle Dutch Hadewart (a personal name) and wout (“marsh forest, swamp forest”). The element dorp (“village”) was added at a later date to distinguish the settlement from Hazerswoude-Rijndijk.

s.def: a village in Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

hazerswoude-rijndijk.e: First attested as hadewartswoude and hadewertswoude in 1280-1287. Compound of Middle Dutch Hadewart (a personal name) and wout (“marsh forest, swamp forest”). The compound element Rijndijk (derived from the hydronym Rijn and dijk (“levee”)) was added at a later date to distinguish the settlement ...

s.def: a hamlet in Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

heimansbuurt.e: Attested as Heimansbuurt in 1957. Compound of the hydronym Heimanswetering and buurt (“hamlet”).

s.def: a hamlet in Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

hogeveen.e: First attested as hoogeveen in 1375. Compound of Middle Dutch hooch (“high, elevated”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

horstenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of horst (“overgrown elevated place”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The names of the neighbourhood's streets all end in the element horst.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

hoge zijde.e: Attested as Hoogen Ryn Dyck in 1746. Derived from hoog (“high, elevated”) and zijde (“bank, edge”). The toponym contrasts with Lage Zijde.

s.def: a hamlet in Bodegraven-Reeuwijk, South Holland, Netherlands

hogebrug.e: Attested as brug in 1615. Compound of hoog (“high, elevated”) and brug (“bridge”). Named after a nearby bridge.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

hoogeveen in rijnland.e: Named after Hogeveen, with the identifier in Rijnland added to distinguish the municipality from Hogeveen near Delft.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

hazerswoude.e: Named after the village of Hazerswoude, currently split into Hazerswoude-Dorp and Hazerswoude-Rijndijk.

habaai.e: Originally the name of a plantation.

hato.e: Borrowed from Papiamentu Hato, potentially derived from Spanish hato (“ranch”). The coastal strip was a suitable location for cattle ranching.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Aalsmeer, North Holland, Netherlands

hornmeer.e: Attested as Bedykte Stom en Horn Meer in 1749. Derived from a hydronym, derived in turn from a compound of horn (“corner”) and meer (“pool”).

s.def: a hamlet in Gooise Meren, North Holland, Netherlands

hakkelaarsbrug.e:
Attested as Hackelaers brug in 1617. Likely a compound of hakkelaar (“stutterer”) and brug (“bridge”). Named after a former toll bridge.
Compare Stokkelaarsbrug.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Gooise Meren, North Holland, Netherlands

het spiegel.e: Attested as Het Spiegel around 1734. Derived from spiegel (“mirror”). Named after a piece of land shaped roughly like a convex mirror.

s.def: a hamlet in Huizen, North Holland, Netherlands

huizerhoogt.e: Compound of the toponym Huizen and hoog (“height, elevated place”) with epenthetic -t. The settlement is located on top of a hill.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Huizen, North Holland, Netherlands

huizermaat.e: First attested as huysermaet in 1442. Compound of the toponym Huizen and a variant of Middle Dutch made (“meadow, pasture”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hilversum, North Holland, Netherlands

hilversumse meent.e: Attested as Hilversumsche Meent in 1865. Derived from the toponym Hilversum and meent (“communal land”).

s.def: a hamlet in Wijdemeren, North Holland, Netherlands

hinderdam.e: First attested as hinderdam in 1317. Derived from Middle Dutch hinderdam (“dam constructed to prevent one watercourse from linking up with another”).

s.def: a hamlet in Wijdemeren, North Holland, Netherlands

horstermeer.e: Attested as Horster Meer in 1639. Compound of the toponym Nederhorst den Berg and meer (“lake”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Weesp, North Holland, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

holysloot.e: First attested as holensloot in 1343. Compound of an inflected form of Middle Dutch hol (“low-lying, deep, swampy”) and sloot (“ditch”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

haarlemmerbuurt.e: Univerbation of Haarlemmer (“of or pertaining to Haarlem”) + buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood is named after a street leading to Haarlem.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

helmersbuurt.e: Compound of the name of Jan Frederik Helmers and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

holendrecht.e: First attested as helendrecht in 1328. Compound of Middle Dutch hol (“low-lying, deep, swampy”) and -drecht (“ferry, watercourse”). Named after a river.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

het funen.e: Derived from Danish Funen. Named after the Danish island.

s.def: a hamlet in Waterland, North Holland, Netherlands

het schouw.e: Attested as Schou in 1647. Derived from schouw (“ferry”).

s.def: a hamlet in Waterland, North Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Zaanstad, North Holland, Netherlands

havenbuurt.e:
* (Waterland) Attested as De Buert in 1680. Compound of haven (“harbour”) and buurt (“hamlet, small settlement”).
* (Zaanstad) Derived from havenbuurt (“harbour neighbourhood”).

s.def: a village in Edam-Volendam, North Holland, Netherlands

hobrede.e: Attested as Hobreet in 1639. Derived from Middle Dutch hobreede (“the short side of a parcel abutting a levee”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Alkmaar, North Holland, Netherlands

huiswaard.e: Attested as Huys waert in 1665. Compound of huis (“house”) and waard (“land next to water, riverine island, polder complex surrounded by water”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zaanstad, North Holland, Netherlands

haaldersbroek.e: Attested as Hallaerts Broeck in 1680. Compound of the toponym De Haal and broek (“marsh, marshland”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zaanstad, North Holland, Netherlands

hembrug.e: Attested as Hembrug in 1899. Compound of the toponym Hem (derived from ham (“alluvial land in the bend of a watercourse”)) and brug (“bridge”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zaanstad, North Holland, Netherlands

hoornseveld.e: First attested as thorne vierendeel and dat hornre vierendeel in 1408. Compound of the toponym De Horn (derived from Middle Dutch horn (“sharp bend, corner”)) and veld (“field”). De Horn was a hamlet and former administrative and legal district of Oostzaan.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zaanstad, North Holland, Netherlands

het kalf.e: Attested as opt Calff in 1582. Likely derived from kalf (“sloughing land”). Originally an independent hamlet.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Haarlemmermeer, North Holland, Netherlands

haarlemmerliede.e: First attested as haerlemmerlee in 1343. Univerbation of Haarlem (“of or pertaining to Haarlem”) + Middle Dutch lede (“dug or canalised watercourse”). Named after a former river.

s.def: a hamlet in Haarlemmermeer, North Holland, Netherlands

huigsloot.e: Attested as Huykesloot in 1594. Derived from a hydronym, derived in turn from Middle Dutch hucslote (“ditch with a barrier”).

s.def: a former municipality of North Holland, Netherlands
a former municipality of North Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Haarlem, North Holland, Netherlands

heiliglanden.e: Named after a pair of streets.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Haarlem, North Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Haarlem, North Holland, Netherlands

haarlemmerhout.e: First attested as haerlem by den houte in 1311. Univerbation of Haarlemmer (“of or pertaining to Haarlem”) + Middle Dutch hout (“rising deciduous forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Velsen, North Holland, Netherlands

hofgeest.e: Attested as hofghieste in 1298. Compound of Middle Dutch hof (“manor”) and geest (“elevated sandy land”).

s.def: a hamlet in Heemskerk, North Holland, Netherlands

heemskerkerduin.e: Attested as Heemskercker Duyn in 1680. Compound of the toponym Heemskerk and duin (“dune”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Heemskerk, North Holland, Netherlands

hoogdorp.e: First attested as hoghe dorpe and hoge dorp in 1251. Derived from a compound of Middle Dutch hooch (“high, elevated”) and dorp (“village, secondary settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Castricum, North Holland, Netherlands

heemstee.e: First attested as hemstede around the end of the 11th century. Derived from Old Dutch hemstede (“homestead”).
